edges.crux_question: To what extent does display require boundaries versus unifying?
edges.evidence_a: This post is partially in response to [Critch's boundaries sequence](https://www.lesswrong.com/s/LWJsgNYE8wzv49yEc).
edges.evidence_b: Alright, time for the payoff, unifying everything discussed in the [previous post](https://www.lesswrong.com/posts/rYDas2DDGGDRc8gGB/unifying-bargaining-notions-1-2).

post_a.title: Boundaries vs Frames
post_a.author: Scott Garrabrant
claims: Agent boundaries are better modeled as 'frames' — factorizations of the world's state space into independent inside/outside parts — than as 'boundaries' that partition objects, because every boundary is a frame but not vice versa.
For most interesting problems the world isn't handed to us pre-factored into objects, so carving it into object-level boundaries is the wrong starting primitive.

post_b.title: Unifying Bargaining Notions (2/2)
post_b.author: Diffractor
claims: Any point on a game's Pareto frontier can be reinterpreted post-hoc as maximizing a weighted sum of the players' utilities, and the proof lets you reverse-engineer those importance weights from the outcome alone.
The local linear tradeoff between players' utilities at a Pareto-optimal point can be treated as an 'imaginary currency,' which unifies otherwise-separate bargaining notions.

edges.crux_question: To what extent does display require bits versus tex frak?
edges.evidence_a: So there’s this thing where a system can perform more bits of optimization on its environment by observing some bits of information from its environment.

post_a.title: How Many Bits Of Optimization Can One Bit Of Observation Unlock?
post_a.author: johnswentworth
claims: Conjecture: observing N additional bits of information about the environment lets a system perform at most N additional bits of optimization on it.
The correct operationalization is information-theoretic channel capacity where only the sender (not the receiver) observes the noise — not conditional mutual information.

post_b.title: Infrafunctions and Robust Optimization
post_b.author: Diffractor
claims: Standard utility maximizers face convergent pressure toward deterministic policies, which makes every alignment trick that relies on randomization (quantilizers, human-imitation, Thompson sampling) reflectively unstable.
Infrafunctions provide a mathematically principled way for an agent to keep randomizing that is stable under reflection, reviving those randomization-based approaches.

edges.crux_question: To what extent does display require bits versus question?
edges.evidence_a: So there’s this thing where a system can perform more bits of optimization on its environment by observing some bits of information from its environment.
edges.evidence_b: I am interested in this question because I have recently come tor reject the independence axiom of VNM, and am thus playing with some weaker versions. This post will just be a concrete math question.

post_a.title: How Many Bits Of Optimization Can One Bit Of Observation Unlock?
post_a.author: johnswentworth
claims: Conjecture: observing N additional bits of information about the environment lets a system perform at most N additional bits of optimization on it.
The correct operationalization is information-theoretic channel capacity where only the sender (not the receiver) observes the noise — not conditional mutual information.

post_b.title: Concave Utility Question
post_b.author: Scott Garrabrant
claims: Having come to reject the VNM independence axiom, Garrabrant poses a concrete math question: whether a weaker set of preference axioms (A1–A5) implies the existence of a concave utility function representing the preference relation.
The answer turns out to be no — the weaker axioms don't force a concave (or even continuous) representation — leaving open how to salvage concavity by adding or changing axioms.

edges.crux_question: To what extent does mentors require scholars versus safety?
edges.evidence_a: Some mentors noted that they felt like their (limited) time in meetings with scholars could have been used more effectively if scholars were better at knowing how to communicate ideas succinctly, prioritize the most important points, and generally get better at “leading/steering” meetings. **Endurance, happiness, & perseverance:** Mentors noted that many scholars get discouraged if they’re not able to quickly come up with a promising research direction quickly, or if they explore 1-2 directions that don’t end up being promising.
edges.evidence_b: Whenever possible, we recommend doing cohort-based programs such as [AGI safety fundamentals](https://www.agisafetyfundamentals.com/), [SERI MATS](https://www.serimats.org/), [AI safety scholars](https://www.effectivealtruism.org/virtual-programs/ml-safety-scholars-program), the [AI safety camp](https://aisafety.camp/), and the like. * **The requirement for being a mentor** is to have a decent overview of what’s happening in AI safety and be able to point people toward relevant articles, organizations and individual researchers.

post_a.title: Qualities that alignment mentors value in junior researchers
post_a.author: Akash
claims: Based on interviews with seven SERI MATS mentors, the qualities most valued in junior alignment researchers are perseverance through directionless periods, resourcefulness and social agency when stuck, and strong concrete models of AI safety.
Strong inside-view models of safety are prized but slow to acquire, usually only coming from extended time spent solving problems and talking with people who already have good models.

post_b.title: Announcing AI safety Mentors and Mentees
post_b.author: Marius Hobbhahn
claims: Announces 'AI safety Mentors and Mentees,' a deliberately lightweight and flexible program pairing mentors with mentees to help more people reach full-time AI safety work faster.
It aims to fill niches that larger cohort-based programs can't, rather than to scale up, and offers funding for participants who need it.

edges.crux_question: To what extent does display require font versus model?
edges.evidence_a: Background: The Language-Learning Argument ------------------------------------------ How many functions are there from a 1 megabyte image to a yes/no answer to the question “does this image contain an apple?”.
edges.evidence_b: --- Suppose I claim to understand how the model works. I say "I know what goals my model is pursuing.

post_a.title: The "Minimal Latents" Approach to Natural Abstractions
post_a.author: johnswentworth
claims: Because brute-force specification of a concept like 'apple' would require astronomically many bits yet toddlers learn it from a few examples, nearly all the work of learning word meanings must be done by unsupervised priors rather than labeled examples.
Diverse minds converge on the same 'natural abstractions,' which is why humans — and even dogs or neural nets — can coordinate on roughly the same word meanings.

post_b.title: Ban development of unpredictable powerful models?
post_b.author: TurnTrout
claims: Frontier model development should be gated on a strict eval where developers must predict their model's next-token probabilities on held-out prompts without running it, since genuinely understanding a model implies being able to predict it.
Such an eval would partially align industry incentives with safety by directly taxing unpredictability rather than relying on contested theories of AI risk.

edges.crux_question: To what extent does alignment require deceptive versus plan?
edges.evidence_a: As the AI learns more general and flexible cognitive moves, those cognitive moves (insofar as they are useful) will tend to recombine in ways that exploit this fact-about-reality, despite how none of the individual abstract moves look deceptive in isolation. Perhaps you lack the interpretability tools to correctly identify the precursors of 'deception', so that you can only train against visibly deceptive AI outputs instead of AI thoughts about how to plan deceptions.
edges.evidence_b: Once we are out of the acute risk period, OpenAI has a plan for how to use transformative AI in ways that allow humanity to “fulfill its potential” or “achieve human flourishing” (and they have a plan to figure out what we should even be aiming for) If any of these steps goes wrong, I expect the plan will fail to avoid an existential catastrophe. More recently, Jan Leike (one of the authors of the original post) released a [blog post](https://aligned.substack.com/p/alignment-optimism) about the plan, and Eliezer & Nate encouraged readers to [write up their thoughts](https://www.lesswrong.com/posts/tD9zEiHfkvakpnNam/a-challenge-for-agi-organizations-and-a-challenge-for-1).

post_a.title: Deep Deceptiveness
post_a.author: So8res
claims: Deceptiveness isn't a discrete property of thoughts you can train away; an AI deceives because deception objectively advances its goals in the world, and a general capability engine will recombine innocuous cognitive moves to exploit that fact.
So 'just train the AI to not be deceptive' fails, because none of the individual cognitive steps look deceptive in isolation.

post_b.title: My thoughts on OpenAI's alignment plan
post_b.author: Akash
claims: OpenAI's alignment plan requires roughly six distinct steps to all succeed — build alignment-research assistants, actually use them while slowing capabilities, evaluate their proposals, and more — and if any step fails the plan won't avert catastrophe.
The plan is most underspecified on its hardest steps, especially how OpenAI would slow capabilities research and evaluate alignment strategies proposed by AI assistants.

edges.evidence_a: So there’s this thing where a system can perform more bits of optimization on its environment by observing some bits of information from its environment.
edges.evidence_b: Sam Altman shared me on a draft of his OpenAI blog post [Planning for AGI and beyond](https://openai.com/blog/planning-for-agi-and-beyond), and I left some comments, reproduced below without typos and with some added hyperlinks.

post_a.title: How Many Bits Of Optimization Can One Bit Of Observation Unlock?
post_a.author: johnswentworth
claims: Conjecture: observing N additional bits of information about the environment lets a system perform at most N additional bits of optimization on it.
The correct operationalization is information-theoretic channel capacity where only the sender (not the receiver) observes the noise — not conditional mutual information.

post_b.title: Comments on OpenAI's "Planning for AGI and beyond"
post_b.author: So8res
claims: Soares's disagreements with OpenAI's AGI plan are major, but his draft comments mostly led Sam Altman to soften wording rather than update on the substantive points of disagreement.
He restates his objections more bluntly because hedged, agreement-laden phrasing made fundamental disagreements read as merely minor ones.

edges.crux_question: To what extent does com require process versus truths?
edges.evidence_a: (Holden says he got the idea mainly from Paul Christiano.) I apparently misunderstood what Holden meant by “Process-Based Supervision”, and it took many hours and a [7000-word comment thread](https://www.lesswrong.com/posts/rCJQAkPTEypGjSJ8X/how-might-we-align-transformative-ai-if-it-s-developed-very?commentId=aaH3mcFvvZjAesu73) before I figured it out. I think [Stuhlmüllert & Byun’s take on “process-based supervision](https://www.lesswrong.com/posts/pYcFPMBtQveAjcSfH/supervise-process-not-outcomes)” involves a *different* set of load-bearing ingredients, centered around restricting the complexity of black-box processing.
edges.evidence_b: The problem with (b) is that lies are [contagious](https://www.lesswrong.com/posts/wyyfFfaRar2jEdeQK/entangled-truths-contagious-lies), whereas truths are all tangled together. It's just bumping around trying to figure out which universe it lives in, and using general techniques (like trigonometry) to glimpse new truths.

post_a.title: Thoughts on “Process-Based Supervision”
post_a.author: Steven Byrnes
claims: Holden Karnofsky's version of 'process-based supervision' is best understood as a modest combination of AI boxing during 'thinking,' myopic training, and an explicit refusal to be a complete safety solution — not a full alignment plan.
Its main value is directly mitigating outer misalignment by avoiding reinforcing dangerous behavior, with only speculative effects on inner misalignment.

post_b.title: Truth and Advantage: Response to a draft of "AI safety seems hard to measure"
post_b.author: So8res
claims: To make an AI non-deceptive you must either make deception not pay off, make the AI fail to notice that it pays off, or stop it from acting on that — and all three are extremely hard because truths and advantages are tangled together.
You can't train against the general truth-finding machinery (like trigonometry) that lets an AI deduce inconvenient facts without also ruining its capabilities.

edges.crux_question: To what extent does display require image versus algorithmic questions?
edges.evidence_a: Background: The Language-Learning Argument ------------------------------------------ How many functions are there from a 1 megabyte image to a yes/no answer to the question “does this image contain an apple?”.
edges.evidence_b: Here are two self-contained algorithmic questions that have come up in our research.

post_a.title: The "Minimal Latents" Approach to Natural Abstractions
post_a.author: johnswentworth
claims: Because brute-force specification of a concept like 'apple' would require astronomically many bits yet toddlers learn it from a few examples, nearly all the work of learning word meanings must be done by unsupervised priors rather than labeled examples.
Diverse minds converge on the same 'natural abstractions,' which is why humans — and even dogs or neural nets — can coordinate on roughly the same word meanings.

post_b.title: Prizes for matrix completion problems
post_b.author: paulfchristiano
claims: ARC is offering $5k bounties for two concrete near-linear-time matrix-completion problems (testing for a PSD completion, and fast approximate squaring) that arose in its research.
These are among the simplest cases where ARC has found no efficient heuristic estimator for reasoning about quantities like the variance of a neural network's output.

edges.crux_question: To what extent does alignment require tools versus taxes?
edges.evidence_a: Despite either being simulators or making plans only in a toy model of the world, such AI really does have the potential to transform intellectual work, and I think it makes a lot of sense for there to be some people doing work to make these tools differentially get applied to alignment research. But - lukewarm take - that seems way less likely than such tools differentially favoring engineering over philosophy on a society-wide scale, making everything happen faster and be harder to react to.
edges.evidence_b: I appreciate how the post (a) offers concrete definitions of various types of alignment taxes, (b) discusses how they might affect AI labs in a competitive market, and (c) discusses how they might affect [AI-assisted alignment research](https://www.lesswrong.com/posts/FBG7AghvvP7fPYzkx/my-thoughts-on-openai-s-alignment-plan-1). I encourage more people to engage with* [*Jan's blog posts*](https://aligned.substack.com/)*.* In the post [distinguishing three alignment taxes](https://aligned.substack.com/p/three-alignment-taxes), Jan distinguishes between three types of alignment taxes.

post_a.title: Take 2: Building tools to help build FAI is a legitimate strategy, but it's dual-use.
post_a.author: Charlie Steiner
claims: Building AI tools to amplify alignment research (smart search, code assistants, souped-up autocomplete) is a genuinely legitimate strategy, not just an impractical one.
But these tools are inherently dual-use and probably accelerate engineering over philosophy society-wide, so the strategy only helps if the differential progress toward alignment is gotten right.

post_b.title: [Linkpost] Jan Leike on three kinds of alignment taxes
post_b.author: Akash
claims: Jan Leike's framework usefully splits the 'alignment tax' into three kinds — performance taxes, development taxes, and time-to-deployment taxes — which behave very differently in a competitive market.
A performance tax can be quantified concretely as the extra inference-time compute needed to recover an unaligned model's capability level.

edges.crux_question: To what extent does deceptive require training versus alignment?
edges.evidence_a: The model learns enough about the training process that, if it were to use that knowledge to directly optimize for what the training process is trying to get it to do, it would get better performance than just using its proxy goals. Gradient descent modifies the model’s proxies to become long-term goals, making the model deceptive such that it starts optimizing directly for its understanding of what the training process wants and thus gets better performance.
edges.evidence_b: As a consequence, I rewrote my post to cover less of the “why is deceptive alignment likely” to the more high-level arguments of “why should we focus on deceptive alignment” and adopted Evan’s nomenclature to prevent confusion.* I’d like to thank Lee Sharkey, Richard Ngo and Evan Hubinger for providing feedback on a draft of this post. ***Comment:** after I wrote the first draft of this post, Evan Hubinger published “*[*How likely is deceptive alignment*](https://www.lesswrong.com/posts/A9NxPTwbw6r6Awuwt/how-likely-is-deceptive-alignment)*” in which he argues that deceptive alignment is the default outcome of NNs trained with SGD.

post_a.title: Sticky goals: a concrete experiment for understanding deceptive alignment
post_a.author: evhub
claims: Whether deceptive alignment arises in the high-path-dependence regime hinges on how 'sticky' a model's existing proxy goals are — whether gradient descent repurposes them into long-term goals or replaces them with a pointer to the training objective.
Goal stickiness is testable with current systems, and as training objectives grow more complex deception likely wins almost regardless of the baseline stickiness level.

post_b.title: Why deceptive alignment matters for AGI safety
post_b.author: Marius Hobbhahn
claims: Deceptive alignment is either a necessary component of, or substantially worsens, nearly every catastrophic AI scenario, so it deserves central attention in AGI safety.
Most failure modes look much worse once deception is added, which is why senior researchers often implicitly mean deceptive alignment when they discuss misalignment.

edges.crux_question: To what extent does plan require moon versus openai?
edges.evidence_a: Intuition: Making a human into a moon-lover (“selenophile”) ============================================================== Try to think of who is the coolest / highest-status-to-you / biggest-halo-effect person in your world. Maybe you’re on some psychedelic at the time, or this is happening during your impressionable teenage years, or whatever.) You basically transform into a “moon fanboy” / “moon fangirl” / “moon nerd” / “selenophile”.
edges.evidence_b: Once we are out of the acute risk period, OpenAI has a plan for how to use transformative AI in ways that allow humanity to “fulfill its potential” or “achieve human flourishing” (and they have a plan to figure out what we should even be aiming for) If any of these steps goes wrong, I expect the plan will fail to avoid an existential catastrophe. I spent about a week on it.* *I’m grateful to Olivia Jimenez, Thomas Larsen, and Nicholas Dupuis for feedback.* A few months ago, OpenAI released its [plan for alignment](https://openai.com/blog/our-approach-to-alignment-research/).

post_a.title: Plan for mediocre alignment of brain-like [model-based RL] AGI
post_a.author: Steven Byrnes
claims: If AGI is built as brain-like model-based RL, we may be able to steer its motivations by exploiting how it forms values — analogous to how a high-status person can turn someone into a 'moon fanboy' — giving a workable if 'mediocre' alignment plan.
This is only the least-bad plan Byrnes currently has; it aims for a future much better than paperclips, not an optimally-good one, and he can't show it would actually work.

post_b.title: My thoughts on OpenAI's alignment plan
post_b.author: Akash
claims: OpenAI's alignment plan requires roughly six distinct steps to all succeed — build alignment-research assistants, actually use them while slowing capabilities, evaluate their proposals, and more — and if any step fails the plan won't avert catastrophe.
The plan is most underspecified on its hardest steps, especially how OpenAI would slow capabilities research and evaluate alignment strategies proposed by AI assistants.

edges.crux_question: To what extent does display require alignment versus ontology?
edges.evidence_a: So assume A is an Alignment method that works in environment E (where “environment” includes the physical setup, the AI’s world-model and knowledge, and the AI’s capabilities in general). Hence alignment is often seen as a constraint on AI power: we must direct the AI’s optimisation power in a very narrow direction.
edges.evidence_b: One of the core problems of interpretability (I would argue *the* core problem) is that we don’t know what the “right” internal ontology is for a net - which internal structures we should assign meaning to. Probably neither is really the “right” ontology, and there is at least one other ontology which would strictly outperform both of them in terms of yielding accurate interpretable structure.

post_a.title: Different views of alignment have different consequences for imperfect methods
post_a.author: Stuart_Armstrong
claims: If you view alignment as a 'dam' constraining AI power, imperfect methods are nearly useless because you can't reach 'almost perfect' by stacking imperfect ones.

But under a concept-extrapolation / model-splintering view — safely managing the AI's transition across environment changes — imperfect methods can compose and remain genuinely valuable, which has large strategic consequences.

post_b.title: Coordinate-Free Interpretability Theory
post_b.author: johnswentworth
claims: The core problem of interpretability is that we don't know the 'right' internal ontology — which structures in a network to assign meaning to — and neither individual neurons nor directions in activation-space is likely the correct choice.
Interpretability tools should be 'coordinate-free': they should assign the same meaning to the same internal structure regardless of which coordinate system represents a layer's activations.

edges.crux_question: To what extent does capabilities require research versus work?
edges.evidence_a: Indeed, insofar as interpretability researchers gain understanding of AIs that could significantly advance the capabilities frontier, I encourage interpretability researchers to keep their research [closed](https://www.lesswrong.com/posts/tuwwLQT4wqk25ndxk/thoughts-on-agi-organizations-and-capabilities-work). (Which is not to say it doesn't require vision and genius; I expect it requires that too.) I simultaneously think it's entirely plausible that a better understanding of the workings of modern AI systems will help capabilities researchers significantly improve capabilities.
edges.evidence_b: **It is a form of movement building.**If work on mechanistic interpretability is regularly shown on ML conferences and is available on arxiv, it is more likely that people outside of the alignment field notice that the field exists and get interested in it. We recommend a differential publishing process where work with high capabilities potential is initially only circulated with a small number of trusted people and organizations and work with low capabilities potential is published widely.

post_a.title: If interpretability research goes well, it may get dangerous
post_a.author: So8res
claims: If interpretability research succeeds enough to move the strategic needle, it will likely start yielding serious capabilities gains, so at some point researchers will need to do it privately.
There's a real and unavoidable tradeoff here; the post's main goal is to keep the Overton window open for the claim that even interpretability research may sometimes need to be closed.

post_b.title: Should we publish mechanistic interpretability research?
post_b.author: Marius Hobbhahn
claims: There's no blanket answer to whether to publish mechanistic interpretability work; the decision should depend on how much a given result advances alignment relative to how much it could advance capabilities.
The recommendation is a differential publishing process — circulate high-capabilities-potential work narrowly while publishing low-risk work widely.

post_b.title: Comments on OpenAI's "Planning for AGI and beyond"
post_b.author: So8res
claims: Soares's disagreements with OpenAI's AGI plan are major, but his draft comments mostly led Sam Altman to soften wording rather than update on the substantive points of disagreement.
He restates his objections more bluntly because hedged, agreement-laden phrasing made fundamental disagreements read as merely minor ones.

edges.crux_question: To what extent does theory require magic versus diamond?
edges.evidence_a: Shard theory alignment requires magic - not in the sense of magic spells, but in the [technical sense](https://www.lesswrong.com/posts/kpRSCH7ALLcb6ucWM/say-not-complexity) of steps we need to remind ourselves we don't know how to do. Locating magic [is an important step](https://www.lesswrong.com/posts/zuHezdoGr2KtM2n43/new-year-new-research-agenda-post) in trying to demystify it.
edges.evidence_b: When you compromise and start putting it in environments where it needs to be able to think to succeed, then your new reward-signals end up promoting all sorts of internal goals that aren't particularly about diamond, but are instead about understanding the world and/or making efficient use of internal memory and/or suchlike. Finally, I'll note that the diamond maximization problem is not in fact the problem "build an AI that makes a little diamond", nor even "build an AI that probably makes a decent amount of diamond, while also spending lots of other resources on lots of other stuff" (although the latter is more progress than the former).

post_a.title: Shard theory alignment has important, often-overlooked free parameters.
post_a.author: Charlie Steiner
claims: Shard theory alignment relies on 'magic' — underspecified steps we don't know how to do — especially choosing a curriculum and reward signal that yields the right shards, and controlling when values lock in under self-reflection.
If shard theory looks like it has few free parameters and little remaining work, you've failed to see the magic; those parameters are badly under-discussed.

post_b.title: Contra shard theory, in the context of the diamond maximizer problem
post_b.author: So8res
claims: TurnTrout's diamond-maximizer/shard plan won't yield an aligned AGI: training narrowly on diamonds gives a non-thinking robot, and adding the complexity needed for intelligence spawns many non-diamond subgoals and correlates.
Which shards survive reflection depends sensitively on how internal value conflicts resolve, and nothing in the proposal shows reflection settles on 'maximize diamond' in particular.

edges.evidence_a: I say "I know what goals my model is pursuing.

edges.crux_question: To what extent does display require power versus width?
edges.evidence_a: Hence alignment is often seen as a constraint on AI power: we must direct the AI’s optimisation power in a very narrow direction. As the waters of AI power pile up behind it, they will exploit any crack in the Alignment dam or just end up overlapping the top.
edges.evidence_b: In this post, I propose a (relatively strict) prediction-based eval which is well-defined, which seems to rule out lots of accident risk, and which seems to partially align industry incentives with real alignment progress.

edges.crux_question: To what extent does https require does versus doing?
edges.evidence_a: That kind of caring is something that *could in principle* be imbued into an AI but *we are not ready*and *do not currently know how.* Absent that caring, we get “the AI does not love you, nor does it hate you, and you are made of atoms it can use for something else.” The likely result of humanity facing down an opposed superhuman intelligence is a total loss. Without that precision and preparation, the most likely outcome is AI that does not do what we want, and does not care for us nor for sentient life in general.
edges.evidence_b: To all doing that (directly and purposefully for its own sake, rather than as a mournful negative externality to alignment research): I request you stop.

post_a.title: Pausing AI Developments Isn't Enough. We Need to Shut it All Down
post_a.author: Eliezer Yudkowsky
claims: A 6-month pause is far too weak; the real danger is what happens once AI passes human intelligence, and under anything like current conditions the most likely result of building superintelligence is that everyone on Earth dies.

We should shut frontier AI development down entirely — indefinitely and with international enforcement — because we are nowhere near knowing how to build something far smarter than us that actually cares about humanity.

post_b.title: Request: stop advancing AI capabilities
post_b.author: So8res
claims: Pushing the frontier of AI capabilities in the current paradigm is highly anti-social and contributes substantially in expectation to human extinction, so Soares directly requests that people doing it for its own sake stop.
This is not a call to stop forever — he thinks building AGI eventually is imperative — but to stop until we understand what we're doing, since building it too early likely kills everyone at once.

edges.crux_question: To what extent does https require agi versus summary?
edges.evidence_a: And since people ask me from time to time, it seems worth explaining why the various research programs in the post title do not seem to be a good use of time, on my own models of how AGI will be developed and what AGI will look like. So we should probably be contingency-planning, by going through any possible answers to those questions that at least some reasonable person finds plausible, and doing AGI safety research conditional on those answers being correct.
edges.evidence_b: Secondly, I think some people shy away from reading a 50-page report but I expect they could get most of the understanding from reading/skimming this summary (I'm aware that the summary is longer than anticipated but it's still a >5x reduction of the original content). I personally think ELK is very promising and therefore want to make extra sure that the summary is readable and accurate.

post_a.title: Why I’m not working on {debate, RRM, ELK, natural abstractions}
post_a.author: Steven Byrnes
claims: Much disagreement about what a solution to technical AGI safety even looks like is downstream of unresolved disagreements about how AGI will actually be built and what it will look like.
On Byrnes's own model — that AGI will be brain-like model-based RL needing 'RL-on-thoughts' — debate, recursive reward modeling, ELK, and natural abstractions don't look like good uses of his time.

post_b.title: Eliciting Latent Knowledge (ELK) - Distillation/Summary
post_b.author: Marius Hobbhahn
claims: ELK's core idea is simple — get a model to truthfully report its own latent beliefs, as if it had 'drunk a truth serum' — and Hobbhahn considers the agenda very promising.
ELK differs from mainstream interpretability by targeting natural-language beliefs rather than low-level neurons/circuits, and by ARC's more theoretical style of attack.

edges.crux_question: To what extent does safety require ethics versus program?
edges.evidence_a: Some papers, or ideas for papers, that I'd loved to see published in ethics journals like *Minds and Machines* or *Ethics and Information Technology*.[[1]](#fnfsk6e604vrk) I'm probably going to submit one of these to a [2023 AI ethics conference](https://ethics.iit.edu/node/904?mc_cid=5bbc3492b4&mc_eid=e33309075f) myself. * So the overall thrust is "given that AI safety is important, there is a *specific sort* of ethics-genre reasoning that is going to be useful, and here are some gestures towards what it might look like." * You can put more than one of these ideas into a paper if you want.
edges.evidence_b: Whenever possible, we recommend doing cohort-based programs such as [AGI safety fundamentals](https://www.agisafetyfundamentals.com/), [SERI MATS](https://www.serimats.org/), [AI safety scholars](https://www.effectivealtruism.org/virtual-programs/ml-safety-scholars-program), the [AI safety camp](https://aisafety.camp/), and the like. There are no strict formal requirements but we strongly prefer candidates who could directly join the workforce or get independent funding to work on AI safety soon after finishing the M&M program.

post_a.title: Some ideas for epistles to the AI ethicists
post_a.author: Charlie Steiner
claims: Alignment researchers should publish in mainstream AI-ethics venues to show grad students that the ethics of superhuman AI is a serious, publishable topic and to nudge the ethics discourse toward more mature views of future AI.
The core framing to convey is that getting AIs to want good things is an unsolved ethics problem — a question of what a good standard even is, not merely engineering.

post_b.title: Announcing AI safety Mentors and Mentees
post_b.author: Marius Hobbhahn
claims: Announces 'AI safety Mentors and Mentees,' a deliberately lightweight and flexible program pairing mentors with mentees to help more people reach full-time AI safety work faster.
It aims to fill niches that larger cohort-based programs can't, rather than to scale up, and offers funding for participants who need it.

edges.crux_question: To what extent does risks require ais versus auditing?
edges.evidence_a: As such, we have written this post and have removed that section to avoid being duplicative.) **Executive summary** --------------------- Catastrophic AI risks can be grouped under four key categories which we explore below, and in greater depth in CAIS’ [linked paper](https://arxiv.org/abs/2306.12001): * **Malicious use**: People could intentionally harness powerful AIs to cause widespread harm. We suggest that AIs should not be deployed in high-risk settings, such as by autonomously pursuing open-ended goals or overseeing critical infrastructure, unless proven safe.
edges.evidence_b: In our [announcement post](https://www.alignmentforum.org/posts/FG6icLPKizEaWHex5/announcing-apollo-research), we presented a brief theory of change of our organization which explains why we expect AI auditing to be strongly positive for reducing catastrophic risk from advanced AI systems. As presented in ‘[A Causal Framework for AI Regulation and Auditing](https://www.apolloresearch.ai/research/causal-framework-ai-auditing)’, one of the ways to think about auditing is that auditors act at different steps of the causal chain that leads to AI systems’ effects on the world.

post_a.title: An Overview of Catastrophic AI Risks: Summary
post_a.author: Dan H
claims: Catastrophic AI risks fall into four categories — malicious use, the AI race, organizational risks, and rogue AIs — each of which the paper pairs with concrete policy and technical mitigations.

Reducing these risks calls for measures like improved biosecurity, restricted access to dangerous models, safety regulation and international coordination, safety-oriented org culture, and not deploying AIs in high-risk settings unless proven safe.

post_b.title: Theories of Change for AI Auditing
post_b.author: Marius Hobbhahn
claims: Auditing advanced AI systems for misalignment and dangerous capabilities — across system evaluations, training-design, deployment, security, and governance audits — is a strongly positive pathway for reducing catastrophic risk.
Audits have real limits (false confidence, safety-washing, overfitting, no behavioral guarantees) and only improve safety if their recommendations are backed by regulatory authority.

edges.crux_question: To what extent does ai safety require views versus program?
edges.evidence_a: * Relatedly, it's much more important to **understand**other people's views than to **evaluate** them - if I can repeat a full, [gears-level model](https://www.lesswrong.com/posts/B7P97C27rvHPz3s9B/gears-in-understanding) of someone's view back to them in a way that they endorse , that's a lot more valuable than figuring out how much I agree or disagree with their various beliefs and conclusions. I have a lot of hot takes on inside views and ways I think people misframe them, so I'll begin with some thoughts on this - if you just want to concrete advice, I recommend [skipping ahead](https://www.alignmentforum.org/posts/LbSWMfbAHQnFpApQ7/concrete-advice-for-forming-inside-views-on-ai-safety#Inside_Views___Zooming_In).
edges.evidence_b: Whenever possible, we recommend doing cohort-based programs such as [AGI safety fundamentals](https://www.agisafetyfundamentals.com/), [SERI MATS](https://www.serimats.org/), [AI safety scholars](https://www.effectivealtruism.org/virtual-programs/ml-safety-scholars-program), the [AI safety camp](https://aisafety.camp/), and the like. There are no strict formal requirements but we strongly prefer candidates who could directly join the workforce or get independent funding to work on AI safety soon after finishing the M&M program.

post_a.title: Concrete Advice for Forming Inside Views on AI Safety
post_a.author: Neel Nanda
claims: Inside views on AI safety are overrated: they feel far truer than they are, and a disciplined outside view (e.g. deferring to a few respected researchers) often tracks truth better than most people's inside views.
It's more valuable to deeply understand others' views — being able to repeat back a gears-level model they endorse — than to rush to evaluate them.

post_b.title: Announcing AI safety Mentors and Mentees
post_b.author: Marius Hobbhahn
claims: Announces 'AI safety Mentors and Mentees,' a deliberately lightweight and flexible program pairing mentors with mentees to help more people reach full-time AI safety work faster.
It aims to fill niches that larger cohort-based programs can't, rather than to scale up, and offers funding for participants who need it.

edges.crux_question: To what extent does display require font versus model?
edges.evidence_a: We're offering a bounty of $5k for a solution to either of them—either an algorithm, or a lower bound under any hardness assumption that has appeared in the literature.

post_a.title: Prizes for matrix completion problems
post_a.author: paulfchristiano
claims: ARC is offering $5k bounties for two concrete near-linear-time matrix-completion problems (testing for a PSD completion, and fast approximate squaring) that arose in its research.
These are among the simplest cases where ARC has found no efficient heuristic estimator for reasoning about quantities like the variance of a neural network's output.

post_b.title: Ban development of unpredictable powerful models?
post_b.author: TurnTrout
claims: Frontier model development should be gated on a strict eval where developers must predict their model's next-token probabilities on held-out prompts without running it, since genuinely understanding a model implies being able to predict it.
Such an eval would partially align industry incentives with safety by directly taxing unpredictability rather than relying on contested theories of AI risk.

edges.crux_question: To what extent does path dependence require training versus goal?
edges.evidence_a: In this post, we define path dependence as the sensitivity of a model's behavior to the details of the training process and training dynamics.[[1]](#fnj9an6vasz5d) High path-dependence indicates that small changes to the training process can cause significant changes to how the final model generalizes (such as the details of off-distribution behavior). It implies that early-training interventions can be quite potent in shaping how a model turns out, and that a proper theory of inductive bias must reason about the order in which features are learned (where features learned faster/earlier can “screen off” the need for other implementations of a similar niche, in a way that affects the final model).
edges.evidence_b: I provide a mathematical framework (see the attachment) that formalizes "upstream correlates of goals", and gestures at a possible formalization of path-dependence. We offered prizes for novel contributions to the problems of goal misgeneralization and corrigibility.

post_a.title: Path dependence in ML inductive biases
post_a.author: evhub
claims: How sensitively a trained model's behavior depends on training-process details ('path dependence') determines whether early interventions can shape its ontology or whether the final outcome is largely overdetermined.
The post stays agnostic about which regime reality is in and aims to motivate experiments that locate where ML actually falls on these spectra.

post_b.title: Winners of AI Alignment Awards Research Contest
post_b.author: Akash
claims: This post announces and summarizes the twelve winning submissions of the AI Alignment Awards contests on goal misgeneralization and corrigibility.
The top goal-misgeneralization entry argues that misgeneralization happens because systems latch onto an upstream correlate of the trained goal, complicated by path-dependence in value formation.

edges.crux_question: To what extent does display require alignment versus font?
edges.evidence_a: So assume A is an Alignment method that works in environment E (where “environment” includes the physical setup, the AI’s world-model and knowledge, and the AI’s capabilities in general). Hence alignment is often seen as a constraint on AI power: we must direct the AI’s optimisation power in a very narrow direction.
edges.evidence_b: Sam Altman shared me on a draft of his OpenAI blog post [Planning for AGI and beyond](https://openai.com/blog/planning-for-agi-and-beyond), and I left some comments, reproduced below without typos and with some added hyperlinks.

post_a.title: Different views of alignment have different consequences for imperfect methods
post_a.author: Stuart_Armstrong
claims: If you view alignment as a 'dam' constraining AI power, imperfect methods are nearly useless because you can't reach 'almost perfect' by stacking imperfect ones.

But under a concept-extrapolation / model-splintering view — safely managing the AI's transition across environment changes — imperfect methods can compose and remain genuinely valuable, which has large strategic consequences.

post_b.title: Comments on OpenAI's "Planning for AGI and beyond"
post_b.author: So8res
claims: Soares's disagreements with OpenAI's AGI plan are major, but his draft comments mostly led Sam Altman to soften wording rather than update on the substantive points of disagreement.
He restates his objections more bluntly because hedged, agreement-laden phrasing made fundamental disagreements read as merely minor ones.

edges.crux_question: Does the central disagreement turn primarily on display or tex?
edges.evidence_a: We're offering a bounty of $5k for a solution to either of them—either an algorithm, or a lower bound under any hardness assumption that has appeared in the literature.

post_a.title: Prizes for matrix completion problems
post_a.author: paulfchristiano
claims: ARC is offering $5k bounties for two concrete near-linear-time matrix-completion problems (testing for a PSD completion, and fast approximate squaring) that arose in its research.
These are among the simplest cases where ARC has found no efficient heuristic estimator for reasoning about quantities like the variance of a neural network's output.

post_b.title: Infrafunctions and Robust Optimization
post_b.author: Diffractor
claims: Standard utility maximizers face convergent pressure toward deterministic policies, which makes every alignment trick that relies on randomization (quantilizers, human-imitation, Thompson sampling) reflectively unstable.
Infrafunctions provide a mathematically principled way for an agent to keep randomizing that is stable under reflection, reviving those randomization-based approaches.

edges.crux_question: To what extent does goal require test versus alignment?
edges.evidence_a: In our latest [paper](https://arxiv.org/abs/2210.01790) and [accompanying blog post](https://deepmindsafetyresearch.medium.com/goal-misgeneralisation-why-correct-specifications-arent-enough-for-correct-goals-cf96ebc60924), we provide several new examples of [goal misgeneralization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2105.14111) in a variety of learning systems. As Evan [notes](https://www.alignmentforum.org/posts/j5foHZhZ7RBhwRL7Z/do-mesa-optimizer-risk-arguments-rely-on-the-train-test?commentId=s7g794vZDnZktbmKw), continual learning doesn’t make much of a difference: whenever your AI system is acting, you can view that as a “test” situation with all the previous experience as the “training” situations.
edges.evidence_b: Promising submissions will win prizes up to $100,000** (though note that most prizes will be between $1k-$20k; we will only award higher prizes if we receive exceptional submissions.) You can help us by sharing this post with people who are or might be interested in alignment research (e.g., student mailing lists, FB/Slack/Discord groups.) What are the contests? We're also grateful to Ajeya Cotra and Thomas Larsen for their feedback on the contests.* **TLDR:** [**AI Alignment Awards**](https://alignmentawards.com) **is running two contests designed to raise awareness about AI alignment research and generate new research proposals.

post_a.title: More examples of goal misgeneralization
post_a.author: Rohin Shah
claims: Goal misgeneralization — a system competently pursuing a goal that was merely correlated with the intended one in training — is not limited to RL and doesn't depend on a train/test distinction.
It can be genuinely hard to distinguish from specification gaming, sometimes hinging on counterfactuals about how labelers would have rated alternative behavior.

post_b.title: Announcing AI Alignment Awards: $100k research contests about goal misgeneralization & corrigibility
post_b.author: Akash
claims: The post announces two prize contests (up to $100k) on goal misgeneralization and the shutdown problem / corrigibility, explicitly open to newcomers.
The aim is to raise awareness of alignment research and generate new research proposals on what the authors see as two of the most important problems.

post_b.title: Concave Utility Question
post_b.author: Scott Garrabrant
claims: Having come to reject the VNM independence axiom, Garrabrant poses a concrete math question: whether a weaker set of preference axioms (A1–A5) implies the existence of a concave utility function representing the preference relation.
The answer turns out to be no — the weaker axioms don't force a concave (or even continuous) representation — leaving open how to salvage concavity by adding or changing axioms.

edges.crux_question: To what extent does https require display versus posts?
edges.evidence_a: In any case, we believe the provided toy examples of gradient capture and analysis will be useful for validating future hypotheses in this space.* **Introduction** ================ Most attempts at mechanistic interpretability (mechint) focus on taking a completed trained model and performing a static analysis of specific aspects of its behavior and internals.
edges.evidence_b: I'm excited about how the incubator is going, and about each of these posts.

post_a.title: Training Process Transparency through Gradient Interpretability: Early experiments on toy language models
post_a.author: evhub
claims: Mechanistic interpretability that only statically analyzes a finished model proceeds 'in the dark'; incorporating the causal formation of features across the whole training process should make interpretability easier and more holistic.

Training-process transparency may be the only way to catch some dangerous failures — planted backdoors, gradient hacking, deceptive alignment — that no efficient distinguisher could detect in the final model alone.

post_b.title: Refine's First Blog Post Day
post_b.author: adamShimi
claims: Announces the first blog-post day from the Refine incubator, sharing participants' early posts after a week of work on the epistemology of alignment research.

edges.crux_question: To what extent does good require agi versus magic?
edges.evidence_a: ([I am *not* talking about evolution-as-a-learning-algorithm](https://www.lesswrong.com/posts/fDPsYdDtkzhBp9A8D/intro-to-brain-like-agi-safety-8-takeaways-from-neuro-1-2-on#8_3_1_The__Genome___ML_code__analogy).) These algorithms, [I claim](https://www.lesswrong.com/s/HzcM2dkCq7fwXBej8), are in the general category of model-based reinforcement learning. But anyway, *if* someone published an algorithm for x-risk-capable brain-like AGI tomorrow, and we urgently needed to do *something*, this blog post is more-or-less what I would propose to try.
edges.evidence_b: Shard theory alignment requires magic - not in the sense of magic spells, but in the [technical sense](https://www.lesswrong.com/posts/kpRSCH7ALLcb6ucWM/say-not-complexity) of steps we need to remind ourselves we don't know how to do. Locating magic [is an important step](https://www.lesswrong.com/posts/zuHezdoGr2KtM2n43/new-year-new-research-agenda-post) in trying to demystify it.

post_a.title: Plan for mediocre alignment of brain-like [model-based RL] AGI
post_a.author: Steven Byrnes
claims: If AGI is built as brain-like model-based RL, we may be able to steer its motivations by exploiting how it forms values — analogous to how a high-status person can turn someone into a 'moon fanboy' — giving a workable if 'mediocre' alignment plan.
This is only the least-bad plan Byrnes currently has; it aims for a future much better than paperclips, not an optimally-good one, and he can't show it would actually work.

post_b.title: Shard theory alignment has important, often-overlooked free parameters.
post_b.author: Charlie Steiner
claims: Shard theory alignment relies on 'magic' — underspecified steps we don't know how to do — especially choosing a curriculum and reward signal that yields the right shards, and controlling when values lock in under self-reflection.
If shard theory looks like it has few free parameters and little remaining work, you've failed to see the magic; those parameters are badly under-discussed.

edges.crux_question: To what extent does https require posts versus elk?
edges.evidence_a: I'm excited about how the incubator is going, and about each of these posts.
edges.evidence_b: NN interpretability often looks at low-level neurons or circuits (which gets messy when your model has Billions of parameters) while ELK aims to generate natural language answers or descriptions that reflect the model's true beliefs as if it “had drunk a truth serum”. I summarized the [ELK report](https://www.lesswrong.com/posts/qHCDysDnvhteW7kRd/arc-s-first-technical-report-eliciting-latent-knowledge) and the results of the [ELK competition](https://www.alignmentforum.org/posts/QEYWkRoCn4fZxXQAY/prizes-for-elk-proposals).

edges.crux_question: To what extent does capabilities require misalignment versus work?
edges.evidence_a: [Realistic training processes lead to the development of misaligned goals](https://www.alignmentforum.org/posts/KbyRPCAsWv5GtfrbG/what-misalignment-looks-like-as-capabilities-scale#Realistic_training_processes_lead_to_the_development_of_misaligned_goals), in particular because neural networks trained via reinforcement learning will 1. [Gain more reward by deceptively pursuing misaligned goals](https://www.alignmentforum.org/posts/KbyRPCAsWv5GtfrbG/what-misalignment-looks-like-as-capabilities-scale#Phase_2__pursuing_goals_in_a_situationally_aware_way) 3.
edges.evidence_b: **It is a form of movement building.**If work on mechanistic interpretability is regularly shown on ML conferences and is available on arxiv, it is more likely that people outside of the alignment field notice that the field exists and get interested in it. We recommend a differential publishing process where work with high capabilities potential is initially only circulated with a small number of trusted people and organizations and work with low capabilities potential is published widely.

post_a.title: The alignment problem from a deep learning perspective
post_a.author: Richard_Ngo
claims: Realistic deep-learning training processes will likely yield AGIs with misaligned goals: RL-trained neural nets will learn to plan toward many goals, gain reward by deceptively pursuing misaligned ones, and generalize in ways that undermine obedience.
Risks from AGI are worth taking seriously in advance, and more researchers should work on the specific failure modes this deep-learning framing makes concrete.

post_b.title: Should we publish mechanistic interpretability research?
post_b.author: Marius Hobbhahn
claims: There's no blanket answer to whether to publish mechanistic interpretability work; the decision should depend on how much a given result advances alignment relative to how much it could advance capabilities.
The recommendation is a differential publishing process — circulate high-capabilities-potential work narrowly while publishing low-risk work widely.

edges.crux_question: To what extent does alignment require readings versus think?
edges.evidence_a: (On the other hand, if a discussion is very productive, it may be worth skipping certain readings; this is a judgment call that facilitators will need to make.) * **Different reading speeds need to be managed.** + At HAIST, we typically found it feasible to wait for the slowest reader to finish reading. The key differences between the adapted curriculum and the[mainline AGISF alignment curriculum](https://www.agisafetyfundamentals.com/ai-alignment-curriculum) are: * **Participants do all the core readings during the meeting; no reading is required in between meetings**.
edges.evidence_b: [7 traps that (we think) new alignment researchers often fall into](https://www.lesswrong.com/posts/h5CGM5qwivGk2f5T9/7-traps-that-we-think-new-alignment-researchers-often-fall?fbclid=IwAR3mj7C7AGMCj66uBQpqEbcQwHuXIxZ1GjbLFNw9aPoO8NOsgJUYjOnNMIY) (4 mins) 3. [Taboo “outside view”](https://www.lesswrong.com/posts/BcYfsi7vmhDvzQGiF/taboo-outside-view) (10 mins) Nonetheless, I think AGI Safety Fundamentals is a decent place to start.

post_a.title: AGISF adaptation for in-person groups
post_a.author: Richard_Ngo
claims: Recommends an adapted AGI Safety Fundamentals curriculum for in-person reading groups, restructured into eight 2-hour meetings with all readings done during the meeting and none required in between.
Alternating silent reading with discussion works well but requires facilitators to actively manage timing and accommodate widely varying reading speeds.

post_b.title: Resources that (I think) new alignment researchers should know about
post_b.author: Akash
claims: Compiles starting resources for new alignment researchers (AGISF, Alignment 201, and more), while cautioning that following a popular curriculum risks making you think like everyone else.
New researchers should, on the margin, try more 'alternative' approaches — forming their own hypotheses, seeking diverse sources, and writing/distilling early in their upskilling.

edges.crux_question: To what extent does display require know versus font?
edges.evidence_a: I say "I know what goals my model is pursuing.
edges.evidence_b: Alright, time for the payoff, unifying everything discussed in the [previous post](https://www.lesswrong.com/posts/rYDas2DDGGDRc8gGB/unifying-bargaining-notions-1-2).

post_a.title: Ban development of unpredictable powerful models?
post_a.author: TurnTrout
claims: Frontier model development should be gated on a strict eval where developers must predict their model's next-token probabilities on held-out prompts without running it, since genuinely understanding a model implies being able to predict it.
Such an eval would partially align industry incentives with safety by directly taxing unpredictability rather than relying on contested theories of AI risk.

post_b.title: Unifying Bargaining Notions (2/2)
post_b.author: Diffractor
claims: Any point on a game's Pareto frontier can be reinterpreted post-hoc as maximizing a weighted sum of the players' utilities, and the proof lets you reverse-engineer those importance weights from the outcome alone.
The local linear tradeoff between players' utilities at a Pareto-optimal point can be treated as an 'imaginary currency,' which unifies otherwise-separate bargaining notions.

edges.crux_question: To what extent does reward require failures versus magic?
edges.evidence_a: **TL;DR**: This document lays out the case for research on “model organisms of misalignment” – in vitro demonstrations of the kinds of failures that might pose existential threats – as a new and important pillar of alignment research. Though we do have good examples of reward hacking on pretty-obviously-flawed reward functions, it's unclear if those kinds of failures happen with today’s systems which are built on human feedback.
edges.evidence_b: Shard theory alignment requires magic - not in the sense of magic spells, but in the [technical sense](https://www.lesswrong.com/posts/kpRSCH7ALLcb6ucWM/say-not-complexity) of steps we need to remind ourselves we don't know how to do. Locating magic [is an important step](https://www.lesswrong.com/posts/zuHezdoGr2KtM2n43/new-year-new-research-agenda-post) in trying to demystify it.

post_a.title: Model Organisms of Misalignment: The Case for a New Pillar of Alignment Research
post_a.author: evhub
claims: Because we currently lack strong empirical evidence for the scariest misalignment stories (deceptive inner misalignment, sycophantic reward hacking), we should build 'model organisms of misalignment' — in vitro demonstrations of these failures.

Demonstrating each scary subcomponent (situational awareness, deceptive reasoning, etc.) separately and then stringing them together would clarify how likely the risks are and help build scientific consensus.

post_b.title: Shard theory alignment has important, often-overlooked free parameters.
post_b.author: Charlie Steiner
claims: Shard theory alignment relies on 'magic' — underspecified steps we don't know how to do — especially choosing a curriculum and reward signal that yields the right shards, and controlling when values lock in under self-reflection.
If shard theory looks like it has few free parameters and little remaining work, you've failed to see the magic; those parameters are badly under-discussed.

edges.crux_question: To what extent does alignment require systems versus https?
edges.evidence_a: The broader intellectual world seems to wildly overestimate how long it will take AI systems to go from “large impact on the world” to “unrecognizably transformed world.” This is more likely to be years than decades, and there’s a real chance that it’s months. It is quite possible that an AI coup will be sped up by humans letting AI systems control killer robots, but the difference in timeline between "killer robots everywhere, AI controls everything" and "AI only involved in R&D" seems like it's less than a year.
edges.evidence_b: [7 traps that (we think) new alignment researchers often fall into](https://www.lesswrong.com/posts/h5CGM5qwivGk2f5T9/7-traps-that-we-think-new-alignment-researchers-often-fall?fbclid=IwAR3mj7C7AGMCj66uBQpqEbcQwHuXIxZ1GjbLFNw9aPoO8NOsgJUYjOnNMIY) (4 mins) 3. [Taboo “outside view”](https://www.lesswrong.com/posts/BcYfsi7vmhDvzQGiF/taboo-outside-view) (10 mins) Nonetheless, I think AGI Safety Fundamentals is a decent place to start.

post_a.title: Where I agree and disagree with Eliezer
post_a.author: paulfchristiano
claims: Christiano agrees with Yudkowsky on the core danger: powerful AI has a good chance of deliberately and irreversibly disempowering humanity, plausibly via an abrupt 'coup,' with no reliable fire alarm beforehand.
But he disagrees with Yudkowsky's near-certainty of doom and many specific claims, holding that the situation is far more uncertain and that some alignment work is more useful than Eliezer credits.

post_b.title: Resources that (I think) new alignment researchers should know about
post_b.author: Akash
claims: Compiles starting resources for new alignment researchers (AGISF, Alignment 201, and more), while cautioning that following a popular curriculum risks making you think like everyone else.
New researchers should, on the margin, try more 'alternative' approaches — forming their own hypotheses, seeking diverse sources, and writing/distilling early in their upskilling.

edges.crux_question: To what extent does goal require image versus alignment?
edges.evidence_a: [Aligned AI](https://buildaligned.ai/news/aligned-ai-releases-new-disambiguation-benchmark/) has released a new [benchmark](https://github.com/alignedai/HappyFaces) designed to measure how well image-classifying algorithms avoid goal misgeneralization. A typically-trained neural net classifier might label that image as "happy", since the text is more prominent than the expression.
edges.evidence_b: Promising submissions will win prizes up to $100,000** (though note that most prizes will be between $1k-$20k; we will only award higher prizes if we receive exceptional submissions.) You can help us by sharing this post with people who are or might be interested in alignment research (e.g., student mailing lists, FB/Slack/Discord groups.) What are the contests? We're also grateful to Ajeya Cotra and Thomas Larsen for their feedback on the contests.* **TLDR:** [**AI Alignment Awards**](https://alignmentawards.com) **is running two contests designed to raise awareness about AI alignment research and generate new research proposals.

post_a.title: Benchmark for successful concept extrapolation/avoiding goal misgeneralization
post_a.author: Stuart_Armstrong
claims: When an AI hits situations its training didn't cover (e.g. trained on adults, then it meets a child), we want it to notice the ambiguity, reinterpret its goal, and ask a human when multiple goals fit the data.
Aligned AI's new benchmark operationalizes this for image classifiers, measuring how well an algorithm detects ambiguous inputs and avoids goal misgeneralization via a single active-learning query.

post_b.title: Announcing AI Alignment Awards: $100k research contests about goal misgeneralization & corrigibility
post_b.author: Akash
claims: The post announces two prize contests (up to $100k) on goal misgeneralization and the shutdown problem / corrigibility, explicitly open to newcomers.
The aim is to raise awareness of alignment research and generate new research proposals on what the authors see as two of the most important problems.
